Right? Well, so, again, I'm not having a legal conversation with you. Okay? I will tell you, when it's done at this level, a compliance guide goes over it pretty thoroughly. Lawyers make different decisions about the gray areas in which they choose to operate, but for the most part, they have stopped and they stopped just short of the line by which they will be hung. And then there's issues of, do you refund? Do you, all of that, that affect? But what you want to do is go as far as you can. Now, this happens to be a high-risk category. So stuff people eat, or smear on, or suppository into their body, however it is they get it in there, that's a relatively high-risk category. Telling the ordinary moke that he can get rich is a relatively high-risk category. Some people, for example, that the world is going to end, which Dellinger's irritatingly told me this morning, that's not what he really does. But telling people that the world is going to end and the entire power grid is going to be gone and you're all going to starve, by the way, that's not a high-risk category. The regulatory community pretty much doesn't care about that and you can make any predictions you want. So different categories have different risks. But here's another question, Mark. This is a popular technique, by the way. So this pill will put your doctor out of business. That's a step too far. Could this pill put your doctor out of business? Experts say yes. And so this is a good, better best, by the way. This is an inventory load as well. So this is full of statements of profound importance. What it is, they have said everything they can say about themselves, making it as groundbreaking and amazing and miraculous and new as they possibly can. And most people, to your question, most people stop way shorter where they could go legally. They inhibit themselves about this far tighter than what the law will allow. And they inhibit themselves far short of what their audience is willing to believe. They stop themselves short. And you want to go to the outer limit of this that law and you sleeping at night will allow you to go. The other way to get there, back to your page, whatever it is, my list, the other way to get there is with very dramatic analogies. They are their own form of statements of profound importance. And there are different ways to create added importance other than statements. So one that I tend to use a lot that mostly is not actually true, but it doesn't matter, is trademark, register mark, copyright mark. If you have a patent, patent number, if you don't, patent pending, which will be pending forevermore, these things instantly make whatever the hell you've attached them to seem more important. Non-disclosure statements make whatever you attach them to seem more important. The way you deliver things, which we're talking about in a second, it's a hijack of its own, is added importance. So the very fact, for example, so often when I'm talking about direct mail, I'm really talking about FedEx. They should, there should be a statue of me at FedEx headquarters for all of the FedEx packages that I get people to send and that I've sent over the years. Back in the bad old days in the Taliesin seminar, they brought trucks over and parked them out in the hotel parking lot because we had everybody sit in a room like this and make out their FedExs to send the recruiting tapes off to their distant friends, relatives, and neighbors. We had one night of the seminar, was watching videos while you filled out FedExs and we filled trucks, trucks, and they brought trucks over, but they'll bring you one by the way, if you've got enough. They'll bring a truck and leave it there for you to fill up. They're happy to do that. But the very fact that it arrives by FedEx makes whatever's in it more important than if it arrived by mail. It's a very cheap, very reliable hijack. It's a very fast, so first of all, if you're B2B, FedEx is always handled differently than anything else. Half of the direct mail I get has been stuffed in the wrong direction. People open an envelope from the back with the back facing them. They don't open it with the front facing them and morons will load their stuff in with the thing they want to see first facing the front. And often the thing you see first is the order form which they have put at the back. So this thing shouldn't have been, so here it is. No, we're going to go through all this expense and then we're going to have somebody trying to get her on the phone, which never happened. But it starts being good with the envelope. That's how it starts being good, right? It's because it has a better chance of being opened. So, where are we? I did all that. Page 107. This copy is about... This copy is about finding out what your target really wants. That they somehow feel prohibited from buying or doing, embarrassed about wanting that you can make okay. Now, the good news is a lot of people feel intimidated and embarrassed about stuff that is not illicit. It just seems inappropriate. So, the luxury car business was really affected during the earliest years after the 2008 crash and people would enunciate it. I like a new car. I got plenty of money. I want to buy a new car, but I know a couple of my neighbors in my big affluent neighborhood have fallen on hard times. And I kind of don't want to rub their nose in me driving home anew for sales. And they would enunciate this thought of a real salesman. It was a factor suppressing luxury auto sales. So, had the auto industry been really effective from advertising on down to salespeople at making it okay to drive home in the new Mercedes, they would have sold more cars. The best thing is if you can not only make it okay, but you can make it either a badge of courage or a badge of honor. So, you will see me constantly doing this with the very idea of trying to get rich and being rich. Because many people feel it is unseemly. They are uncomfortable with it. But for my purposes, I need you all in lustful pursuit of wealth. It is on my shortlist all the way back to day one conversation. I need you chasing a dream. If you are like complacent, you are not real valuable to be. So, I got to make that constantly okay. Thus, pretty much no months package of newsletters goes out without some dissertation that makes that okay. It may be in one place one month, it may be in another place another month, but I am going to bet, I didn't check, but I am going to bet if you take the last 12 months and you go through all of them, you are going to find somewhere. Me making it very much okay for you to have what I call earned privilege. I gave you, this is from Rob report, I thought this guy did a great job. You can read it on your own. In defense of decadence. It starts out so, while in the throws of preparing this year's best of the best issue, the editorial staff received notification from a certain publicly funded institution that our publication would be banned from the premises due to the decadent and inappropriate nature of its content. Now, that would be much better if you had named the public institution. No balls. But the rest of it is very well written. Couple of ads. So that's that kind of copy. I'm watching the clock a little bit now. Number 16, page 112. This just gets a quick mention, but it's instructive. And that is, at some point, how tough should you be? So if you take a look at, like if you go back and look at Brinkley, the goat testicles guy, and by the way, if you don't have that course package, it's really great. We've got all these archives of his actual stuff. This is his actual doctor book that he used. This is his actual success story about his hospitals. Audio CDs of his radio shows are in there. It's really a great package. But if you go back to Brinkley, he used, like Dan was saying, he just copied. So Brinkley was a sequential series of steps once he had a lead. And they got sterner and sterner as they went along. And ultimately getting really kind of bullying for the people who haven't responded. Well, why not? At some point, you paid to get the lead. What exactly do you have to lose? So at some point, you get rough. And you shame people and you push people. So one of the things you did that now got you to 27%, that they're apologizing to you for, right? Is you shamed them a little bit about not having responded. And now not only did he bump the conversions by 27%, you told me this morning, you're getting what, emails or tweets or whatever the hell you're getting. Yeah. So they're now apologizing for not having done what they should have done, right? Now, when you really know you got it right, the conversion percentage tells us a lot, but is when you keep getting the apologies and you get some pissed off ones, right, who are mad about how heavy handed you were. Now you know you got it about right. I gave you, forget who sent him to me. Oh, Connor sent him to me. Posters, recruiting posters from the UK for World War I. And I really liked so this one. Be honest with yourself. Be certain that your so-called reason is not a selfish excuse. Unlist today. Women of Britain say go. This one. Number three, what will you say in years to come when people ask you, where did you serve in the Great War? What will you answer when your children grow up and say, father, why weren't you a soldier too? That's pretty heavy handed, right? I mean, that's pretty rough. And that is at some point in your marketing process, that is where you want to be. At some point, even if you've been nice to a certain point, you want to turn tough. And you want to make them ashamed and cringing at what they haven't done. Number 17, Rep.
